en: seven-and-a-half million pounds, probable bankrupt proceeding,
imminent collapse of project, well, imminent...
Imminent collapse of project? Humphrey, why didn't you tell me all this before?
Minister, I am deeply conscious of the heavy burdens of your office.
And when this becomes public knowledge, it'll be all over the front pages.
It's a public scandal, a disaster, it's... It's appalling.
Wait a minute, Jim, look. This report is dated before the election.
You're in the clear, you're laughing. Yes, but unfortunately,
under the Convention of Ministerial Responsibility, the blame...
But everyone will know it wasn't Jim. Quite so, but the principle of democratic accountability
requires the occasional human sacrifice, when the pack is baying for blood.
Isn't that right, Minister? Surely he has only to point at the dates.
Oh, a lesser man might try to wriggle out of it, but there is only one honourable course open,
as the Minister is well aware.
Don't you think Frank might have a point? Except, of course, only today, Minister.
You have publicly identified yourself with the success of the project.
My God, the broadcast? Yes, Minister. When's it going out? Well, it's due for transmission about now, Minister.
Now? Yes, I'm afraid so. Get on to the BBC at once! Yes, Minister. Stop it, Bernard!
Stop what, Minister? Oh, yes. Yes, sir. Well, Minister, I wish you luck, but you know what the BBC are like.
This is crisis, an emergency. This is a scandal.
Of course, I'm sure if you were to put it like that, they might.
Might what? Move it to peak listening time.
Any luck, Bernard? I'd just like to point out that there is a perfect job for what can be done.
It's going on up in Solihull now. And government money and private investment in real partnership.
And it does seem to me symbolic of everything this government is working for.
I've taken a great deal of personal interest in the Solihull project.
And it's not just words, it's actually there in print.
Symbolic of everything this government is working for.
Humphrey! How could you let me say all those things?
Minister, I can only advise. I did advise.
I advise most strongly, but when the advice is advised...
All right, all right, all right, all right.
Advise me now. Certainly, Minister.
Now, it is possible that the bank may take over the contract
from Bradley and Sloan Enterprises Limited, and all will be well.
The bank? Fine. Well, the bank, however, is hesitant.
But it so happens that the director in charge is to retire next year
and he's anxious to find another appointment.
A chairmanship of a quango, for instance. Well, give him one.
Give him that one you were reserving for that idiot Desmond Glazebrook.
Who is the director in charge? Desmond Glazebrook.
He's not a bad chap. He's always attacking the government in public.
Yes, well, it does us good sometimes to appoint our opponents.
I mean, you know, it's democratic, statesmanlike. Yes, I've changed my mind. I think that's a very good appointment.
But you can't. Oh, shut up, Frank! Anybody else know about this?
Well, apart from ourselves and the bank and Michael Bradley, only the union representative on the project.
Joe Morgan, of course. That's what was behind his special Birmingham Alliance claim.
Blackmail. That's quite true, Minister. Humphrey, I've been thinking, if Desmond Glazebrook is going to be chairman,
he'll need some support, won't he? I was thinking of a deputy chairman, someone with real experience of industry.
An excellent thought, Minister. Is there anyone you could advise me would be particularly appropriate,
do you think? A trade unionist, for example?
Um, what about Joe Morgan, Minister?
Joe Morgan? Excellent idea. Well done, Humphrey.
Oh, it takes two to quango, Minister.
Thank you.
